oai_dc:dc.dc:title: The 1960s Curriculum Controversy over Measurable Learning Objectives: Historical Perspectives and Enduring Tensions
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Evaniuck, Jayson
Simpson, Timothy L
Everitt, Kevin
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: In the late 1960s, a spirited curriculum debate took center stage within the Curriculum Division of the AERA annual meetings. The academic battleground yielded substantive debate over Measurable Learning Objectives (MLO). This paper explores this notable academic skirmish, tracing the lengthy historical lineage of the MLO movement, arguments advanced by advocates of MLO, and substantive critiques from Elliot Eisner, Harry Broudy, James McDonald, and Robert Ebel. Though MLOs are entrenched in current educational practice, the battle persists between mechanistic educational practices and human-centered approaches. Thus, recalling the earlier debate deepens our perspective on the persistent battle and a commonplace educational practice, learning objectives.
oai_dc:dc.dc:publisher: Society for the Study of Curriculum History
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: Curriculum History; Vol. 2026 No. 1 (2026): Curriculum History 2026
